channel.title: NPR Topics: Science
channel.description: The latest health and science news. Updates on medicine, healthy living, nutrition, drugs, diet, and advances in science and technology. Subscribe to the Health & Science podcast.
channel.copyright: Copyright 2024 NPR - For Personal Use Only

item.title: 2025 saw relatively fewer natural disasters. Will you get a break on home insurance?
item.description: Disaster costs fell in the U.S. in 2025. Still, it was the fourth time in five years that extreme weather inflicted more than $100 billion in annual losses. Industry experts say the growing financial toll will make insurers wary of rushing to cut rates.
item.content:encoded:
Disaster costs fell in the U.S. in 2025. Still, it was the fourth time in five years that extreme weather inflicted more than $100 billion in annual losses. Industry experts say the growing financial toll will make insurers wary of rushing to cut rates.

(Image credit: ALLISON JOYCE/AFP via Getty Images)
item.dc:creator: Michael Copley

item.title: GLP-1s have transformed weight loss and diabetes. Is addiction next?
item.description: A large study found that people taking GLP-1 drugs like Ozempic for diabetes were less likely to be diagnosed with substance use disorder.
item.content:encoded:
A large study found that people taking GLP-1 drugs like Ozempic for diabetes were less likely to be diagnosed with substance use disorder.

(Image credit: Maria Fabrizio for NPR)
item.dc:creator: Jon Hamilton

item.title: Epstein paid for a conference of top scientists in 2006. His motives are now clear
item.description: A 2006 conference for physicists in the U.S. Virgin Islands that included a trip to Jeffrey Epstein's private island shows how he used his wealth to build relationships with prominent scientists.
item.content:encoded:
A 2006 conference for physicists in the U.S. Virgin Islands that included a trip to Jeffrey Epstein's private island shows how he used his wealth to build relationships with prominent scientists.

(Image credit: JPL-Caltech/NASA, Getty Images and Department of Justice)
item.dc:creator: Scott Neuman

item.title: Chimps' taste for fermented fruit hints at the origins of humans' love of alcohol
item.description: Scientists analyzed the urine of wild chimpanzees who'd feasted on fallen fruit to see how much alcohol they consumed from the fermented sugars.
item.content:encoded:
Scientists analyzed the urine of wild chimpanzees who'd feasted on fallen fruit to see how much alcohol they consumed from the fermented sugars.

(Image credit: Sharifah Namaganda)
item.dc:creator: Ari Daniel

item.title: Come along with some geese as they migrate back from their southern winter havens
item.description: Geese's iconic "V" formations and trademark squawks can be seen and heard overhead as they go back and forth to the south through the year. But what does it take for such a long trip?
item.content:encoded:
Geese's iconic "V" formations and trademark squawks can be seen and heard overhead as they go back and forth to the south through the year. But what does it take for such a long trip?

(Image credit: Charlie Neibergall)
item.dc:creator: Ayana Archie

item.title: A new study finds dangerous chemicals in some commonly used hair extensions
item.description: NPR's Ayesha Rascoe speaks to researcher Elissia Franklin about a new study which found dangerous chemicals in some commonly used hair extensions.
item.content:encoded: NPR's Ayesha Rascoe speaks to researcher Elissia Franklin about a new study which found dangerous chemicals in some commonly used hair extensions.
item.dc:creator: Ayesha Rascoe

item.title: How a handful of koalas are changing scientists understanding of genetic risk
item.description: It's long been assumed that koalas in southern Australia are genetically unhealthy. A new study finds they're actually recovering, changing how scientists look at genetic risks.
item.content:encoded: It's long been assumed that koalas in southern Australia are genetically unhealthy. A new study finds they're actually recovering, changing how scientists look at genetic risks.
item.dc:creator: Nathan Rott

item.title: This week in science: Prehistoric cooking, earthquakes in the PNW, and teens' sleep
item.description: Regina Barber and Katia Riddle of NPR's Short Wave podcast talk about prehistoric cooking, earthquakes in the Pacific Northwest and how teens are sleeping less than before.
item.content:encoded: Regina Barber and Katia Riddle of NPR's Short Wave podcast talk about prehistoric cooking, earthquakes in the Pacific Northwest and how teens are sleeping less than before.
item.dc:creator: Katia Riddle

item.title: Colossal Biosciences breeds controversy while trying to revive mammoths
item.description: A Texas biotech company is trying to bring mammoths and other extinct creatures back to life. The science is as intriguing as the ethical questions are thorny.
item.content:encoded:
A Texas biotech company is trying to bring mammoths and other extinct creatures back to life. The science is as intriguing as the ethical questions are thorny.

(Image credit: Rob Stein)
item.dc:creator: Rob Stein

item.title: Humans' pull toward alcohol may have ancient origins (according to chimp pee)
item.description: Scientists learned that wild African chimpanzees consume alcohol by eating fermented fruit, suggesting that human attraction to alcohol may have ancient evolutionary origins.
item.content:encoded: Scientists learned that wild African chimpanzees consume alcohol by eating fermented fruit, suggesting that human attraction to alcohol may have ancient evolutionary origins.
item.dc:creator: Ari Daniel
